title: Mindfulness-based stress reduction for healthy individuals: A meta-analysis
headline: Mindfulness-based stress reduction for healthy individuals: A meta-analysis
summary: A meta-analysis / systematic review of Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) examined emotional wellbeing. Effect-size estimates suggested that MBSR is moderately effective in pre-post analyses (n=26; Hedge's g=.55; 95% CI [.44, .66], p<.00001) and in between group analyses (n=18; Hedge's g=.53; 95% CI [.41, .64], p<.00001). Results suggested large effects on stress, moderate effects on anxiety, depression, distress, and quality of life, and small effects on burnout.
keyFindings: Effect-size estimates suggested that MBSR is moderately effective in pre-post analyses (n=26; Hedge's g=.55; 95% CI [.44, .66], p<.00001) and in between group analyses (n=18; Hedge's g=.53; 95% CI [.41, .64], p<.00001).
Results suggested large effects on stress, moderate effects on anxiety, depression, distress, and quality of life, and small effects on burnout.
limitations: However, heterogeneity was high, probably due to differences in the study design, the implemented protocol, and the assessed outcomes.
Pooled estimates combine heterogeneous studies; effect sizes vary by practice, population, and control condition.
The practice studied (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)) is not the Miracle of Mind practice; findings do not transfer directly to MoM.
